item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's way beyond 200 upvotes, I don't think it's gonna be rewritten anytime soon. Maybe someone can do a tale or something to try and expand on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm genuinely surprised that this hasn't been rewritten yet, considering how it is currently.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Filename: whitealbum-new.jpg
Author: Elogee FishTruck, Rudi Riet
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Source Link: SCP Foundation Wiki
Derivative of:

Name: Three White Albums
Author: Rudi Riet
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Source Link: Flickr

item.title: Just a thought
item.content:encoded:
Since theres really nothing explaining how the disk was made only by who, is this an early member of AWCY.
It'd be awesome it said "stay cool" at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has always lowkey been one of my favorites. The simplicity and lack of explanation are what make it feel genuinely mysterious. It's whimsical yet subtly unsettling, without a clear resolution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love to see this expanded a bit, it's a good foundation (no pun intended, lol) but lacks substance for a really good skip. As it looks now this one feels more like a joke skip to me than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would the authir please gicebus some more in this article? I don't kbow if they're around anymore, but yikes… So short.

item.title: Re: Agree
item.content:encoded:
Like, if you touch it you can only speak in Beatles lyrics

How would this be an improvement? We already have a lot of compulsion-formula SCP articles.

item.title: Agree
item.content:encoded:
It needs some spice.
Like, if you touch it you can only speak in Beatles lyrics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is disc one of the vinyl just an ordinary first disc of The White Album? Because this is a two-disc set, and Revolution 9 isn't 'till side D. Is the first just an ordinary disc apart from being grooveless?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s just because it’s old and has a low number. Newbies read through series I and get to this before understanding what our standard are at now.

All we can do? Downvote and hope others follow suit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm uncultured swine that's missing a reference, but this does nothing for me, and even by early S1 standards, I have no idea how this is rated so highly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite outdated. It’s incredibly simple, lacks substance, and is at its core a fairly basic concept. I get that it’s old, but this one doesn’t particularly do much at all. There’s nothing really going on here. It’s a part of our history I wouldn’t miss.

-1, bleh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Paul is dead" isn't creepy. It's literally just an average conspiracy theory about some famous person dying and being replaced by a clone. People come up with crazy stuff like that all the time, it should be treated with the same lack of respect as the flat earth conspiracy theory gets.

There is nothing about The Beatles that make this SCP creepy. I wouldn't even call it creepy, unsettling is a better word. Anyways, what makes this SCP so unsettling is the fact that it refuses to answer questions about The Beatles. The part at the end definetely suggests that this album was made specially for someone by The Beatles. And if they made this, then they aren't normal musical artists, they are anartists. And who knows what other anamolous art The Beatles made? Chances are this isn't the only anamolous record.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this doesn't really feel like an SCP, there's been some mention of level 1/generic/minor/etc 'anomalous artifacts' in other SCP's and this feels like it belongs with them not as something that needs any containment procedure's beyond just locking it in a box and with no use beyond getting info on the music industry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty much what I was going to say. This hasn't aged well, and 315 does the sapient recording thing a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something to be said for brevity, but this has taken that WAY too far. This seems like a decent item concept- a sentient record with a connection to the Beatles - but it's not at all fleshed out. This is the bare minimum of what qualifies as an SCP entry, and unlike other short articles it never does anything to really hook me. At this point, I feel like the only reason it's still here is nostalgia. For now I'm going to downvote; this is one article which needs to be, if not outright deleted, then at least heavily expanded with something interesting, such as interview snippets with the voice on the record or some kind of interesting recovery details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually hate to say it, but this feels more like an Anomalous Item than SCP. And the worst part, it can be interesting. Its insight into the music industry can be interesting, but is not present or in detail.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Beatles are straight up made of creep. When a friend first told me about the 'mythos' surrounding Paul and all their weird creepy, background shit, I couldn't listen to their stuff anymore for a while because it creeped me the hell out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love This SCP and The Beatles. This SCP creeps me out a bit.
Happy hundredth upvote, 043!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A talking record isn't creepy?! ;-) I think the fact that such a thing exists is informative enough to keep it around. From what I've read elsewhere, it seems easy to think of SCPs in the context of The Foundation, which makes some of them seem a little "meh." BUT that's what I like about this and others like it (e.g. SCP-005). They're creepier if you think about discovering them in your house today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the White Album itself is kind of an SCP… Revolution #9 always gives me nightmares… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been bothering me as well. Not to mention, the point where Revolution 9 was supposed to play is on the second disk, iirc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, more important is the fact that the White Album is two discs. The article seems to imply that there is only one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humorous with a hint of creepiness. +1

EDIT: Humorous because it reminds me of that episode of Courage the Cowardly Dog

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, the fifth elephant.

item.title: Re: Where it was found
item.content:encoded: I'm going to go further then that; most of them SHOULDN'T have where it was found. Unless the author can make it interesting, it drags down the rest of the article - sorta like your grandmother telling the story of where she bought that commemorative plate.

item.title: Re: Where it was found
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This works because it's cut out all the unnecessary detail and gets straight to the hook, which really, really digs into you. Adding anything to it would be A Bad Change.

item.title: Re: Where it was found
item.content:encoded: Not every SCP needs a notation on where found.

item.title: Where it was found
item.content:encoded:
Im new here so feel free to tell me to shut up but maybe put something in like where it was found.

Like:SCP-043 was found by agent * at a auction after it had been put up for auction for the recently deceased major music producer ** **.

So that it could kind of point towards the power it could give someone in the music industry. The knowledge it has could be extremely valuable to someone in the music industry and it could kind of give the SCP more value.

Anyway,I hope you get what I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since it has such a good knowledge of music someone should ask it about the other music related SCPs, specifically SCP-012 because of its music theory knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creeped out by talking to the dead. Or a disembodied something.

There was a Beatles "Paul is dead" thing too, which only adds to it if you knew about it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't need to be. sefirosu200x was just wondering why some people were creeped out by it. I'm wondering that as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit.

Ok, forget I asked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who says SCPs need to be creepy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is an absolutely terrible idea. The last thing that this SCP needs is the power to bend reality. The last thing this wiki needs is a shitty jab at Justin Bieber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The author hasn't been seen in three years, so that kind of question is up to Senior Staff.

Since it's at a comfortable score, I'd bet they'd say "No."

Because, really? Really? *sigh*

If you want, write it up in the sandbox and ask for input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could it maybe be changed so that you can ask the voice to make things happen to people in the music industry?

Like, if you ask the voice, do you think Justin Bieber will end up breaking his neck in a freak yachting accident, the voice will say, "I think that's likely…" and then it'll happen like a week afterwards?

It could also be implied that John Lennon was assassinated due to someone using this ability. Maybe through a change to what the note says.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know anything about The Beatles, so someone explain the creep factor for this? It's just a record that talks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda funny how no item on this website creeps me out more than this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So can we use it to settle what happened to Elvis and Jimmy Hoffa? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, the fifth of four. Anyone here remember their Discworld?

OT, this is one of my favorite items on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No offense, but I find the real Revolution 9 far creepier than this.

Then again, when I first heard it as a kid I was convinced that the radio was possessed, so that may not be entirely fair…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I (and it's rating) tend to think it's fine just the way it is…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might be more interesting if, say, the entity had no knowledge of any events occurring after November 22, 1968 (the release date of the White Album).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Accuracy is always a goal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Revolution 9 is actually the 29th track of the White Album, or the fifth track of side four. Does that matter enough to edit it, or is it too pedantic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bored. Lacks oomph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: However, I will note that the fact that it does NOT play Revolution 9 shows good taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, this one seems to me like a good start that doesn't quite carry through. It's not a threat, and there's really nothing we can learn from it (assuming we're not all that interested in music). The notion that someone or something is capable of making such an object is disturbing, but the object itself is more of a curio.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just vague enough to make the hair on my arms stand up. Wonderful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All the claims of being the fifth Beatle are wrong.

THIS is the fifth Beatle! Hehe.
